skills: reverse dialogue completion (supply the missing QUESTION given the answer, not the reply)
riddle-solving (wordplay clue -> vocabulary answer, e.g. "hands and a face but can't hold or smile" -> a clock)
pragmatic/implied-meaning inference from a short dialogue (what does X mean / what does X decide / X is mainly about)
spatial-preposition reasoning with abstract shapes (next to, under)
transitive comparative-chain reasoning (A<B<C<D from 3 linked comparative sentences) built into a ranked bar graph
multi-turn map/direction reading with ordinal turn-counting ("turn right at the second corner") and a "You Are Here" marker
multi-day weather-forecast table reading (temperature + condition + "feels like" distinction)
sign-to-location inference (where would you see this sign)
picture-to-location inference (where can you buy these items)
cross-cultural comparison reading passage (food-sharing customs, Asia vs West) with a NOT-correct question
holiday fact passage with a variable-date distractor ("the fourth Thursday" vs a fixed calendar date)
purpose-inference from a picture (why are they at the beach)
grammar: question formation across all common wh-/yes-no patterns (tested in reverse, i.e. producing the question)
comparative chains (don't live as long as / live longer than / the longest)
punctuation: colon before a list, semicolon misuse, comma before a question tag
vocabulary_topics: food and drinks (hot chocolate, soda, Pad Thai)
question_types: reverse dialogue completion (fill in the question)
standard dialogue completion (fill in the reply)
multi-turn map/direction reading with ordinal counting
cross-cultural comparison passage with a NOT-correct question
holiday-fact passage with a variable-date distractor
reading_patterns: cross-cultural comparison passage (~70-90 words) contrasting a custom between two cultures/countries, followed by a NOT-correct or according-to-the-passage question
single-fact holiday/culture passage (~50-70 words) with a question testing whether the exact stated frequency/date is understood correctly (avoiding a plausible but wrong fixed-date distractor)
dialogue_patterns: reverse-completion pattern: the reply is given and students must recognise which question elicited it
polite refusal that offers an alternative (Sorry, I'm in a hurry, can you see me at 11?)
humility/gratitude idiom exchange (It's my duty / You're welcome)
process-of-elimination deduction exchange (no sodas available -> deduces the substituted drink)
consent/permission-clarification exchange (Of course not, please do -> she doesn't mind)
correctness-check paraphrase of a short narrated scenario
distractor_patterns: wrong question that would elicit a different (though related) answer, for reverse-completion items
literal/unrelated riddle-answer distractor that shares a surface feature but not the defining one
fixed-date distractor for an event whose date is actually variable (the fourth Thursday, not a fixed day-of-month)
spatial-preposition distractor with shapes in a plausible but incorrect relative arrangement
one-link-broken distractor in a transitive comparative chain (correct on 2 of 3 relations, wrong on the third)
answer_logic: reverse-completion items require working backward from a given answer to identify the uniquely-consistent question form (matching both topic and grammatical question type)
transitive-comparative items require chaining 2-3 stated relations into a single total ranking before matching a graph
pragmatic-inference items require deriving what a speaker means or decides, not just what was literally said
variable-date distractors require recognising when a description ("the fourth Thursday") does not correspond to one fixed calendar date
notes: Exam sat 3 Feb 2568 (BE+1 sitting-date convention), the first year explicitly labelled 'PART 1: Language Use (Items 1-21)' and 'PART 2: Reading (Items 22-32)' in the paper itself. Marks a clear difficulty/sophistication jump: reverse-question dialogue completion and pragmatic-inference items become common (previously rare/absent), plus new item types (riddles, spatial prepositions, transitive comparative chains, cross-cultural comparison passages). 32 items total, ends with 'End of the test' after item 32.
